raw_text: Algonquin College of Applied Arts and Technology Legal and Business Information School of Health and Community Studies Course Number: IRM2002 Applicable Program(s): 1624X03FWO- BIT- Information Resource Management Normative Hours: 60 Hrs Core/Elective: CORE AAL: 1624X03FWO Prerequisites: IRM1002 Prepared by: Helena Merriam, Professor Approval Date: June 2022 Approved By: Jodi Jaffray, Chair Corequisites: N/A Approved for Academic Year: 2022-2023 Course Description Students develop skills in planning and executing information searches and evaluating print and electronic resources. Students learn to locate information on selected topics, compile subject-specific annotated bibliographies and instruct library clients in the use of specialized materials and databases. Relationship To Degree Program Learning Outcomes This course contributes to your program by helping you achieve the following Program Learning Outcomes: 1 Evaluate and apply information sources and services as they relate to: (T,A) a. Software tools and development, network technology, computer programming, web and interface development, and database theory and development b. Information management and digital preservation c. Metadata, indexing and classification d. Legal issues in information technology including copyright and intellectual property, e. Business, project management, marketing and communications 3 Find, interpret and evaluate primary and secondary sources of information and statistical data relevant to information resources and services consistent with current standards in information literacy. (T, A) 4 Apply problem-solving skills to information resources and services in a variety of situations. (T,A) 5 Communicate in written English, including clearly organizing written information, developing lines of argument, analysis and conclusions appropriate to purpose and audience. (A) 7 Organize and present written and spoken information in order to train and teach other people to use information resources appropriate to purpose and audience. (T,A) 9 Demonstrate interpersonal communication skills, including listening, observing and responding in order to effectively answer questions and provide information services to a diverse client base. Students also learn aspects of physical bibliography, the book trade, preservation and conservation of books and how to exhibit such material. Relationship To Degree Program Learning Outcomes This course contributes to your program by helping you achieve the following Program Learning Outcomes: 1 Evaluate and apply information sources and services as they relate to: a. Software tools and development, network technology, computer programming, web and interface development, and database theory and development b. Information management and digital preservation c. Metadata, indexing and classification d. Legal issues in information technology including copyright and intellectual property, e. Business, project management, marketing and communications 4 Apply problem-solving skills to information resources and services in a variety of situations. (A) 5 Communicate in written English, including clearly organizing written information, developing lines of argument, analysis and conclusions appropriate to purpose and audience. (A) 6 Communicate in spoken English, including the ability to give effective presentations appropriate to purpose and audience. Describe the key archival concepts and how they form the foundation for archivists’ and records managers’ work. 2.) Articulate the core principles of archivists and records managers, and how they shape the values of the field 3.) Describe the key concepts of special collections and how they relate to the archives and records management fields 4.) Examine the relationship of archives and records management the greater societal context, and focusing on key issues that form current debates. to Upon completion of this course, you should be able to: • Define the concept of authenticity and the importance it plays in the foundation of archival and records management work. • Define the concept of reliability and the importance it plays in the foundation of archival and records management work. • Define the concept of integrity and the importance it plays in the foundation of archival and records management work. • Explore the theoretical, methodological, and practical aspects of arrangement and description in the archival field. • Explain how the key archival concepts are used and interpreted within archival and records management contexts. . • Summarize the key historical shifts within the development of the archival and records management field from the 19th century to the present within the North American context. • Explain the concept of archival ethics and the importance to the • archival and records management professions. Identify the key legal issues that have shaped the conceptualization of archives and records management within the North American context. • Explore core conservation and preservation issues as they relate to digital and analog records • Recognize how the field of rare books and manuscripts relate to the overarching archival and records management fields. • Explore the historical context of rare books and manuscripts and their relationship to current trends and issues within the special collections field. • Understand the unique aspects of preservation and conversation for rare books and manuscripts and how they have been shaped by technological changes in the information management field as a whole. • Understand exhibitions in special collections, and how they are carried out for rare books and manuscripts. • Explore privacy ethics with regards to making records available for use and the differing perspectives within the archives and records management professions. • Explore determining records value and the how that value is determined within a societal context. • Explore the concept of social justice and how it relates to ethical debates within the archival and records • Explore the concept of community, how it is defined, and how it applies to the archival field. • Explore advances in digital records and how they have affected the processes and conceptualization of the core archival and records management principles and practices. © AlgonquinCollege 2 IRM4001 Algonquin College of Applied Arts and Technology II. Statement on Plagiarism The University Academic Integrity Policy deﬁnes plagiarism as “presenting, whether intentionally or not, the ideas, expression of ideas or work of others as one’s own.” This includes reproducing or paraphrasing portions of someone else’s published or unpublished material, regardless of the source, and presenting these as one’s own without proper citation or reference to the original source. Examples of sources from which the ideas, expressions of ideas or works of others may be drawn from include but are not limited to: books, articles, papers, literary compositions and phrases, performance compositions, chemical compounds, artworks, laboratory reports, research results, calculations and the results of calculations, diagrams, constructions, computer reports, computer code/software, material on the internet and/or conversations. Examples of plagiarism include, but are not limited to: • Any submission prepared in whole or in part, by someone else, including the unauthorized use of generative AI tools • • • • (e.g., ChatGPT); using ideas or direct, verbatim quotations, paraphrased material, algorithms, formulae, scientiﬁc or mathematical concepts, or ideas without appropriate acknowledgment in any academic assignment; using another’s data or research ﬁndings without appropriate acknowledgement; submitting a computer program developed in whole or in part by someone else, with or without modiﬁcations, as one’s own; and failing to acknowledge sources through the use of proper citations when using another’s work and/or failing to use quotations marks. Plagiarism is a serious offence that cannot be resolved directly by the course’s instructor. The Associate Dean of the Faculty conducts a rigorous investigation, including an interview with the student, when an instructor suspects a piece of work has been plagiarized. Penalties are not trivial. They can include a ﬁnal grade of “F” for the course. Includes: Experiential Learning Activity COURSE DESCRIPTION AND OBJECTIVES: This introductory course provides students with an overview of the Canadian business environment, the functional areas within a business and introduces skills needed to be an effective employee and manager. Students will learn to recognize and analyze an organization as an integrated entity. This course introduces students to the fundamentals of Problem Solving, Business Communications, Marketing, Entrepreneurship, Operations Management, Management Information Systems, Accounting, Finance, Management and Human Resources. Upon completion, students will be able to demonstrate an understanding of the business concepts as a foundation for introduction into the business environment LEARNING OBJECTIVES: 1 | Demonstrate an understanding of an integrated business and the contribution each unit makes to the success of a business entity in a global society. | Comprehend and use common business terms and strategies used to advance organizational goals. Identify what a business represents and the essential business concepts such as products, services, profit and stakeholders used within an organization. Identify and understand the functional areas: Research and Development; Marketing; Production; Finance – of a business and how they work together for business success. 2 | Demonstrate an understanding of the factors involved in providing a product or service. | List types of consumer and organizational products available for production and sale. List the stages of the life cycle of a product; Identify inputs and outputs; and the core functions in production management. Explain the why quality control and total quality management are important elements of production and their impact on products or services 3 | Explain the role of social responsibility and business ethic in a business environment. | Understand what it means to practice good business ethics. List the factors that influence ethical decisions Discuss the role of business in protecting the natural environment 4 | Develop basic business communication and professional skill-sets. | List the major components of a communication strategy and its importance to all stakeholders. Review various management reports and financial statements that would best communicate the companies positions. Apply the stages of team development and examine why conflict may arise and strategies that can be used to resolve them. REQUIRED READINGS: TEXTBOOK: Nickels, William G., McHugh, James M., McHugh, Susan M., Cossa, Rita, Stevens, Julie (2022), Understanding Canadian Business 11th Edition, McGraw-Hill Ryerson Limited, ISBN10: 1260881369 (Hard copy or digital) Additional reading material and weblinks will be posted on Brightspace. INSTRUCTOR Dr. Emily Hiltz (she/her/hers), Associate Professor, Teaching stream Office Location 4212 Richcraft Hall Email emily.hiltz@carleton.ca Please use your Carleton email and use COMS 2004B in the email subject line. Student Office Hours Tuesdays, 2:00-3:00 pm via Zoom, or by appointment. To join, please see the Office Hour module on the Brightspace course page for the meeting link and passcode. Teaching Assistants Aseel Qazzaz (she/her/hers); facilitating Groups 1 & 3 via Zoom. See Brightspace for link Email: aseelqazzaz@cmail.carleton.ca Grace Goldbaum (she/her/hers); facilitating Groups 2 & 4. See Brightspace for link Email: gracegoldbaum@cmail.carleton.ca Course Description This course teaches foundational research skills and concepts used in the Communication and Media Studies field. Students learn about research traditions in the field, the research process, how to access and evaluate a variety of sources and what procedures and tools are often used to collect and analyze data. This introduction to research prepares students for applied research practice in a variety of contexts. Course Specifics Learning Outcomes By the end of the course, students will be able to: Understand fundamental approaches to Communication & Media Studies research Comprehend how theory, method, data, and knowledge production connect Identify different sources and evaluate their credibility and use for research goals Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative research approaches Develop communication and research skills by accessing, summarizing, evaluating, and citing a variety of sources Course Materials Students are not required to purchase any textbooks or other learning materials for this course. Required weekly readings, such as articles, book chapters, and other learning materials are provided through the MacOdrum Library’s Reserves, accessible through our course Brightspace (BTS) page (see ‘Ares’ listed in the Tools dropdown menu). For the full list of materials assigned each week, please see the schedule on pp. 4-6. The schedule is also posted within each weekly module on our BTS course page. Required Software: Microsoft Word is required to complete assignments in this course. Both are free and accessible for varied devices. To install Microsoft Office 365, which includes Word, please see this link. Course Modality We meet for a weekly ‘live’ or synchronous lecture online over Zoom at 8:35-10:25 am. The labs are also online and follow the lecture at 10:35 am (for Groups 1 and 3) and 11:35 am (for Groups 2 and 4). The tutorials are drop-in sessions to connect with your TA and ask questions about course content and assignments. Complete Quiz #12 by Apr. 7, 11:59. Course Policies Email Policy: When contacting the Teaching Team, please use ‘COMS 2004B’ in the subject line and use your cmail.carleton.ca email address. File Submission locations: All assignment submissions, except for the quizzes, will be made through designated submission areas on Brightspace. To submit your assignments, go to the Assignments module to access the respective instructions and submission portals. The quiz responses are also submitted on BTS in the Quiz section. Required Submission Format: For all written assignments, you are required to use Word to write and complete your work. The Word file format is .doc or .docx. Do not complete the writing in another program (e.g., Pages, Google Docs). We would like to be able to see the editing history of the Word file to see your writing development process. If this is not available, please make sure you have draft versions of your paper saved. Carleton provides free student access to Microsoft 365 that includes Word, and the program is accessible for Mac, Chromebook, and Microsoft users. For more information on how to download the subscription see here. Please do not submit files that are saved as .pdf, .pages or other file formats – they will not be accepted. How your work will be evaluated: For all assessments, marking rubrics are used to assess key criteria such as accuracy, comprehension and application of concepts, argumentation, writing clarity and citation usage. In this class, the citation format is APA 7th ed. A link to recommended APA 7 citation guides is provided on Brightspace. Rubrics are shared with the assignment instructions. Marked rubrics are designed to give you feedback on areas of excellence and improvement regarding your submitted work. The marking team will provide you with written comments along with the rubric rankings. Reviewing and using our feedback is an important element of learning and development in the course. Return of course work Please note it will likely take about 1.5-2 weeks to complete the marking for larger written assignments. The teaching team works diligently in providing constructive feedback you can use to make improvements over the course of the term. Late Assignments & Appeals Policy on late papers and requests for extensions For written assignments, an additional 24 hours is available to submit work if needed -- no late penalty will be applied. If you need more than a 24-hour extension due to illness or bereavement, please do the following: Notify Emily by email before the original assignment deadline, asking for an extension and then complete the Academic Consideration for Coursework Request form by submitting it online. Please only reach out to Prof. Hiltz (Emily) with extension requests, not the Teaching Assistants. Late penalty policy and Cut-Off for Submitting Late Work If the Academic Consideration request form is not completed before an assignment deadline, late work will be penalized one letter grade per day late (e.g., an A paper will be evaluated at an A-), including weekends. Late work will be accepted within 7 days of the original deadline, as outlined in the syllabus. After 7 days, late work will not be accepted. However, if a student has reached out to Emily regarding the need for an extension and completed the Academic Consideration form, a longer extension may be possible depending on individual circumstances. Grade Appeal Policy Please make sure to take some time to review the written comments and rubric details before reaching out with a request to informally appeal the mark. I recommend taking at least 24 hours to review the feedback, the instructions, and your own work. If you still do not agree with the mark, please email me (Prof. Hiltz) with your request to review the paper. Please provide me with a short rationale that specifies where and why the work deserves re-evaluation and a higher mark. Tests and Examinations There are no tests or examinations in the course. Attendance Attending lectures is essential to your learning and skills development. Please be aware that attendance is not monitored. Nonetheless it is important to attend the online lectures and the tutorials in order to get the most out of the class. University policies Diversity Statement Carleton University supports an inclusive learning environment where diverse communities and perspectives are recognized and respected. Our goal as a community is to always ensure a safe learning environment that welcomes open and honest dialogue. We do not allow any form of discrimination, including but not limited to those based on color, age, race, religion, disability, gender, gender identity, gender expression and sexual orientation. Faculty and students are expected to commit to creating a learning environment that encourages inquiry and self-expression, while also demonstrating diligence in respecting how other students may have different viewpoints than their own. Declining Online Imaging and Recording Statement This course uses the video conferencing platform. Zoom. This raises some important privacy considerations. To foster an active learning environment where you and your classmates are engaged in class, I prefer if you occasionally use your camera and/or microphone to communicate (e.g., during class discussion and when asking questions in office hour and tutorial). However, you are not required to turn your camera on and may decide to turn it on or off at any time. Classes and tutorials will not be recorded Statement on Plagiarism The University Academic Integrity Policy defines plagiarism as “presenting, whether intentionally or not, the ideas, expression of ideas or work of others as one’s own.” This includes reproducing or paraphrasing portions of someone else’s published or unpublished material, regardless of the source, and presenting these as one’s own without proper citation or reference to the original source. Examples of sources from which the ideas, expressions of ideas or works of others may be drawn from include but are not limited to: books, articles, papers, literary compositions and phrases, performance compositions, chemical compounds, artworks, laboratory reports, research results, calculations and the results of calculations, diagrams, constructions, computer reports, computer code/software, material on the internet and/or conversations. Examples of plagiarism include, but are not limited to: any submission prepared in whole or in part, by someone else, including the unauthorized use of generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT); using ideas or direct, verbatim quotations, paraphrased material, algorithms, formulae, scientific or mathematical concepts, or ideas without appropriate acknowledgment in any academic assignment; using another’s data or research findings without appropriate acknowledgement; submitting a computer program developed in whole or in part by someone else, with or without modifications, as one’s own; and failing to acknowledge sources through the use of proper citations when using another’s work and/or failing to use quotations marks. A note on the use of generative AI in this class: Any use of generative AI tools to produce assessed content is considered a violation of the academic integrity standards outlined above. Do not use generative AI tools to create topic ideas, write, and/or edit any material you are submitting in this course. A note on resubmitting substantially the same piece of work: You are not allowed to submit work in this class that you completed for another course. You may not re-use material from another section of this class (e.g., if you have previously taken COMS 2004 in another term and are now retaking the course). Communication and Media Studies does not allow students to turn in work that has been submitted for academic credit more than once without permission from their instructors. Examples of unauthorized resubmission of work might include but are not limited to submission of the same paper, written passages, arguments, or ideas submitted for academic credit to another class. Minor changes of phrasing or addition of new written passages to existing work is not enough to constitute new work. Please contact your instructor if there is any question about whether your submission of coursework constitutes a violation of the policy. raw_text: Carleton University Fall 2025 POLM 3000A / COMS 3100A / PSCI 3410A Introduction to Political Management Instructor Instructor: Professor Stephen Azzi Phone: Email: 613-520-2600, ext. 3115 stephen.azzi@carleton.ca Office: Office Hours: Mon., 11:30 a.m.–12:30 p.m., 2435R Richcraft Hall Wed., 1:00–2:00 p.m. (please make an appointment) Course Description This course provides students with an introduction to some key topics in the field of political management, including ethics, strategic communications, media relations, speechwriting, polling, and defamation. Upon completion of this course, students should be able to: Learning Outcomes identify effective and ineffective approaches to political communications; think critically about political management approaches used in and around government; • • define the concept of strategic communications and understand its role in politics; • • describe the main ethical systems and apply them to contemporary political problems; • recognize a variety of rhetorical techniques that add power to speeches and make them more memorable; • explain the concept of framing in a political context, describe George Lakoff’s theory of framing, and identify both effective and ineffective efforts at framing; • describe the basics of defamation law and apply the law in specific cases; • understand the principles of media relations; • organize and participate in a news conference; and • explain the concept of public opinion and the basics of public-opinion polling. Evaluation Speech Analysis I (due 17 September) Group Assignment on Ethics (17 September) Speech Analysis II (due 8 October) Group Assignment on Defamation (1 October) News Conference Simulation (12 or 19 November) Framing Assignment (26 November) Examination 5% 6% 6% 6% 20% 25% 32% You must complete all components of the course to receive a passing grade. 2 POLM 3000 / COMS 3100 / PSCI 3410, FALL 2025 Book There is one mandatory book for this course: George Lakoff, Don’t Think of an Elephant: Know Your Values and Frame the Debate, 2nd ed. (White River Junction, Vermont: Chelsea Green, 2014) (9781603585941). I have asked the Carleton bookstore to order the book. Paperback copies (both new and used) and electronic versions can be purchased from Amazon, Indigo, and ThriftBooks. An electronic copy of the book is available through the Carleton library. Students are expected to read the book at their own pace during the term but are encouraged to do so early in the term. To help pace you, I have suggested in the course schedule which pages you should read each week. Reading the book is essential for writing the framing assignment and answering one mandatory question on the final examination. An online summary of the book is not a substitute for the book itself. There will be one optional—but highly-recommended—discussion of the book on 15 October. This class will be open to anyone who has finished reading pp. 1–82 of the book and is willing to complete the necessary work to prepare for the discussion. If you wish to take part in the discussion, upload to Brightspace a single-spaced, 2- to 3-page document containing your notes on the most important points in the book and 3–5 possible exam questions related to the book. The document must be submitted by 11:55 P.M. on 1 October. You will then receive a list of possible exam questions submitted by your colleagues. Prepare answers to the questions and upload them to Brightspace by 11:55 P.M. on 8 October. Speech Analysis I Find a great speech written in a style you would like to emulate. (For American speeches, a good place to look is www.americanrhetoric.com.) What matters is how speakers presented their views, not whether you agree with them. The speech must have been originally written in English, it must be political, and it must be at least 1000 words long. (You may choose a bilingual Canadian speech if at least half the text is in English.) You will need access to the complete electronic text. Do not choose one of the speeches that we will analyze in class: Martin Luther King’s 28 August 1963 speech at the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, Barack Obama’s 27 July 2004 speech at the Democratic National Convention, or Arnold Schwarzenegger’s 31 August 2004 speech to the Republican National Convention. In your written assignment, provide the internet address for the text of the speech and explain in 150–300 words why you think it is a great speech. Illustrate your answer with quotations from the speech. The emphasis here is on your opinion. Do not seek guidance or quote authorities on what makes for an effective speech. Do not summarize the speech. Do not analyze the message of the speech. Do not just describe the speech—be explicit about why you like it. POLM 3000 / COMS 3100 / PSCI 3410, FALL 2025 3 You should focus not on what the speaker said but on how the speech was written. You are analyzing a written text to understand speechwriting techniques, not watching a video to assess speaking techniques or policy positions. Do not talk simply about the impact of the speech (e.g., “This was a great speech because it was powerful and moving”). Talk about how the speechwriter achieved that impact. You will receive a mark of zero on this assignment if: • at any point in your explanation, you say (or hint) that the speech is great because you agree with its message; the speech is not in English; the speech was translated to English from another language; the speech is shorter than 1000 words; the speech was not political (if in doubt, ask); • • • • • you do not provide an internet address for the complete electronic text; or • you chose one of the speeches that we will be analyzing in class (see list above). Assignments will be graded according to the following criteria: • The assignment should be clearly written in grammatically correct sentences, without bombast or academic jargon. It should have a logical structure, and the ideas should flow clearly. • There should be no spelling or serious grammatical errors. • The assignment should have been prepared according to the course outline and style guide. • The student should have given a personal opinion on why the speech is great. The assignment should be submitted on Brightspace no later than the start of class on 17 September. If you are going to submit the assignment late, you must let me know in writing (in a document uploaded to Brightspace in place of the speech analysis) before the deadline. If you have done so, there will be no penalty, so long as the assignment is ultimately received by start of class on 24 September. If I have not uploaded a document to Brightspace (either the assignment or a document explaining that you will submit the assignment late) by the deadline, you will receive a zero on the assignment. Assignments submitted on time may be revised and resubmitted, so long as the revised version is received by 5 December, the last day of term. Speech Analysis II Mark up the great speech that you selected in your first assignment, identifying the various techniques employed by the speechwriter, and submit it with a checklist that you will find on Brightspace. (An example of how to mark up the speech will be posted on Brightspace.) The assignment will receive a zero if it does not include the checklist or if the speech does not meet the criteria above under “Speech Analysis I.” 4 POLM 3000 / COMS 3100 / PSCI 3410, FALL 2025 The assignment will be graded according to the following criteria: • The assignment should correctly identify at least one example of each rhetorical • technique. If the student claims that there no examples of a particular technique, the technique should not be present in the speech. The assignment must be submitted no later than the start of class on 8 October. If you are going to submit the assignment late, you must let me know in writing (in a document uploaded to Brightspace in place of the assignment) before the deadline. If you have done so, there will be no penalty, so long as the assignment is ultimately received by the start of class on 15 October. This assignment may be submitted on Brightspace or in hardcopy at my office. If the office is closed, please slide the assignment under the door and send me an e-mail to ask for confirmation that I have received it. Any assignments submitted to the offices of the Department of Political Science or the School of Journalism and Communication (or anywhere other than to my office or to me personally) will be assessed a 100 per cent late penalty. If you have not submitted the assignment or uploaded a document to Brightspace by the deadline, you will receive a zero on the assignment. Assignments submitted on time may be revised and resubmitted, so long as the revised version is received by 5 December, the last day of term. News Conference Simulation Students will be divided into groups to simulate a news conference in class on 12 and 19 November. There will not be enough time in class to cover every group, so I will be asking for some groups to volunteer to hold their news conferences earlier or later in the day on one of those dates. You will be given opportunities during class time to plan for the simulation but should also expect to spend time outside of class working with your fellow group members. Afterward, you will be asked to submit a confidential review of your contribution and the contribution of your fellow group members to the news conference, which must be submitted within one week. Your grade will be based on the quality of your group’s news conference, your contribution to the group (according to your own assessment and that of your peers), and your review of your peers. Framing Assignment Write 1500–2500 words to analyze the two main competing sides in an election or referendum campaign and to explain how each side attempted to frame itself, its opponent, and the election issues. Did each side follow George Lakoff’s guidelines for framing? Was the successful side the one that was better at framing? Do not choose the 2016 or 2024 US presidential elections, which will be discussed in class. POLM 3000 / COMS 3100 / PSCI 3410, FALL 2025 5 Your judgements must be based on evidence. Your sources will be any communication that a campaign used to frame an issue, party, or individual, including: • statements quoted in newspaper or magazine articles, • • advertisements (whether on television, Facebook, YouTube, or elsewhere), • social-media posts, and • information on campaign or party websites. remarks reported on radio or television, Particularly useful will be online advertisements, major speeches, and statements in debates. You are free to cite secondary sources (i.e., journalists or scholars who are giving their views on the campaign) but all of your judgements should be backed up with primary sources (material from the campaign or the candidate). The assignment will be marked according to the following criteria: • Understanding – The assignment should show an understanding of the concept of framing as explained in the Lakoff book. • Research – The assignment should be based on extensive research. Every judgement should be backed up with evidence. The citations must help the reader find your sources easily. • Argument – The assignment should answer the question about whether the successful side was the one that had made better use of framing techniques. The answer to this question should be convincing and based on evidence. • Presentation – The assignment should look and sound like it was prepared by a professional. It should be written in a clear and logical style. It should be free of grammatical and typographical errors. The sources should be cited in an appropriate style. The assignment should be formatted according to the guidelines in the course outline and style guide. Note that assignments will receive a grade of zero if they do not contain citations, if the citations do not help the reader find the source easily (e.g., if a citation to a video does not include a time stamp for the relevant portion or if a citation to a multipage work does not include a page number), or if the citations are inaccurate or refer to sources that do not exist. The assignment must be submitted on Brightspace no later than the start of class on 26 November. You would be wise to complete the assignment well before that time in case you encounter a last-minute crisis preventing you from submitting it. If you are going to submit the assignment late, you must let me know in writing (in a document uploaded to Brightspace in place of the assignment) before the deadline. If you have done so, there will be no penalty, so long as the assignment is received by 5 December, the last day of term. If you have not uploaded a document to Brightspace (either the assignment or a document explaining that you will submit the assignment late) by the deadline, you will receive a zero on the assignment. 6 POLM 3000 / COMS 3100 / PSCI 3410, FALL 2025 Group Assignments on Ethics and Defamation You will be divided into your groups for two in-class case studies, one on ethics (17 September) and one on defamation (8 October). You will be evaluated on the quality of your group’s conclusion and on your contribution to the group. For the defamation case, you will function as a panel of judges to rule on the case: is it libel or not? You should try to come to a unanimous ruling. If you cannot agree, you may break into two groups, each producing its own report. For the ethics case, you will function as an advisory group, giving a politician or political aide advice on how to handle an ethical dilemma. Every student will submit a report by the end of class. All students who are a party to a ruling or opinion must have their names on that document. Examination The examination will be three hours in length and will cover material from the lectures and the book. The exam is a test of your mastery of the course material. It is not an exercise to see how clever you are or how much you knew before you took this course. You must engage with the course content, directly confronting the readings and lectures. If you disagree with the course material, you must explain why, offer an alternative interpretation, and provide evidence to support your view. You cannot simply ignore some or all of the course material. Exam questions must be answered. They are not simply themes for you to discuss in general terms. If a question includes sub-questions, you must answer all of them. Here are some exam questions: (1) In politics, what does strategic communications mean? What makes strategic communications strategic? How do strategic communications look in practice? (2) What does Donald Trump’s electoral success say about the traditional rules of political communications? (3) Explain the concept of big data in politics. What is big data? Why is it important? What light does research on political psychology shed on the value of big data? How do campaigns use big data? Textbook: Introduction to Probability and Statistics (4th Canadian Edition by Mendenhall, Beaver, Beaver, and Ahmed). Note: The textbook is required for practice problems. Any edition of the textbook is acceptable. Course description: This course covers statistical data analysis with an emphasis on hypothesis testing including parametric tests (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) and non-parametric tests (e.g., Kruskal-Wallis, Friedman, chi-square), correlation and linear regression. Provides an introduction to probability theory and distributions (e.g. binomial, normal). SPSS will be the statistical software package used. Prerequisites: Restricted to students in the BIT degree program. Evaluation: Your final grade will be calculated as: • Term Mark (60%) o Tests (50%) o Assignments (5%) o Tutorials (5%) • Final Examination Mark (40%) Assignments and tutorials There will be 5 assignments and 5 tutorials, each counting equally toward the term mark. Jan 24,25 5.1 – 5.2,6.1 – 6.4 The Binomial and Normal distributions. Jan 31, Feb 2 7.1 – 7.6 Sampling plans. Sampling distributions of statistics. Central Limit Theorem. Small-sample confidence interval for the difference between two population means (independent samples). Large-sample confidence interval for the difference between two population (binomial) proportions. 9.1 – 9.3, 9.5, 10.3 Testing hypotheses about population parameters. Statistical tests of hypothesis. Type I and Type II errors, power of the test. Large-sample test about a population mean. Small-sample test about a population mean. Large-sample test about a population (binomial) proportion. 9.4, 9.6, 9.7, 10.4, 10.4 Large-sample test of hypothesis for the difference between two population means. Large-sample test of hypothesis for the difference between two population (binomial) proportions. Small-sample inference for the difference between two populations means (paired samples). 11.1-11.3, 11.9 The analysis of Variance: The completely Randomized Design: One- Way Classification. 12.1-12.3 Linear Regression and Correlation. 12.4-12.5 Linear Regression and Correlation Continued 14.1-14.3 Analysis of Categorical Data: Chi square Goodness of fit. raw_text: BIT 2400A (0.5 credits) Intermediate Programming Fall 2022 Instructor: David Sprague Office: AP 230A Email: Phone: Office Hours: david.sprague@carleton.ca (613) 520-2600 x 1324 Tuesdays & Thursdays 10am-11am (AP 230A) Lecture: Tuesdays & Thursdays 16:05-17:25pm Location: NI 4020 Tutorial/Lab: Lab A1 Fridays 14:35 - 17:25 Lab A2 Fridays 8:35 - 11:25 Lab Location: AP 134 TA: TBD Course Description: Introduction to object-oriented programming and algorithm design in C++. Topics include code and data encapsulation using classes and objects, inheritance, polymorphism, object-oriented design, data and code abstraction, program efficiency, user interface objects, event-driven systems, and an introduction to linked-lists and searching. Prerequisites: BIT 1400 Course Objectives/Learning Outcomes: 1. By the end of this course, you will be able to implement new classes and objects in C++ that use inheritance and polymorphism and be able to articulate the benefits of object-oriented design. 2. By the end of this course, you will be able to reason about and use several code design patterns such as event-driven designs, model-view controllers, factory classes, and templated classes. 3. You will discover how standard template library classes, functions, and data structures are used to solve programming challenges quickly (code reuse and conceptual abstractions). 4. During this course you will demonstrate improved programming, debugging, and problem solving (algorithmic thinking) skills when using C++ 5. By the end of this course you will practice using Microsoft Foundation Classes to create simple graphic user interfaces (GUIs) with a GUI editor and minimal coding 6. By the completion of this course, you will write clear, clean object-oriented design code that streamlines peer understanding. You should also be able to justify your coding decisions. (recommended) A discord server (https://discord.com/download) will be set up for peer help, simple questions, and informal messaging. The link will be provided on the LMS. o o Lectures from previous terms can be provided on a per-request basis. • Other Online Resources o http://www.cprogramming.com o http://www.learn-c.org o https://www.onlinegdb.com/online_c_compiler ------------- Tuesdays 09/20/2022 09/27/2022 10/04/2022 10/18/2022 11/08/2022 11/15/2022 11/22/2022 12/06/2022 ----------- 09/22/2021 11/17/2021 TBA % 5 30 6 3 3 6 6 3 3 6 10 5 20 30 Due Date Grading Scheme: Deliverable In-class quizzes (Poll Everywhere) Assignment (top 5 of 6)* A1 C Review A2a Reading Strings 1 A2b Reading String 2 A3 Basic Objects A4 Inheritance and polymorphism A5a MFC A5b MVC and events A6 STL and sorting Lab Submissions (top 10 of 12) Exam 1 Exam 2 Final Exam * Some assignments are broken into two parts (ex A2a and A2b). We drop 1 full assignment or two half-assignments. Assignment submissions due dates may need to change based on your schedules. I am aiming for Tuesday night due dates. Assessments: Course assessments are progressive. 1. Formative quizzes and in-class exercises prepare you for the labs. 2. Labs directly prepare you for assignments. 3. Assignments directly prepare you for the exams. 4. Exams demonstrate that you understand the material. Course Policies: The course is held on-campus and in-person. You are expected to attend and take notes during these times. Schedule: Students are responsible for staying on schedule for the quizzes, labs, and assignments. Work is distributed through the term to spread out your workload. You should expect approximately 10 hours of work (in class and outside of class) per week. Quizzes: Students will take regular in class quizzes to evaluate their understanding of new topics, help cement new knowledge, break up a lecture and promote reflection. • A subset of total quiz questions (approximately 3-4) will be dropped to handle stress weeks, absence due to illness, and other life events. • Polls will be conducted using Poll Everywhere and require you to attend lecture. Class Time Exams: Two exams (30 minutes and 80 minutes) will be held during lecture time. • All exams during the term are taken during the lecture period. All work must be your own. • PMC students (discussed below) need to reach out to me to ensure they have the support they need. • Each student should prepare a double-sided letter-sized “cheat sheet” for the exam and include • whatever they want on the sheet. This sheet helps you study and answer efficiently. If you miss an exam, it is your responsibility to reach out to me as soon as you know you will miss it (or have missed it) so we can figure out a fair policy. • Memorization questions won’t be found on the exam. We are evaluating the learning objectives above with a special emphasis on algorithmic thinking and problem solving. Final Exam: The final exam will take place in the final exam period and be scheduled by the university. It is designed to evaluate all the course learning objectives. It will probably be in person. Assignments: Assignments are given every 1-2 weeks on Tuesdays and are done individually or with a partner • Assignment reflections are due at the same time. We won’t grade your assignment without it. • Assignments can only be submitted via the LMS and late assignments will be given a 0. This is a strict policy. You can submit multiple times so submit early and submit often to avoid this issue. o We also drop at least one full assignment for this reason. • Assignments that don’t compile will be highly penalized. If your code doesn’t compile, comment it out (don’t delete it) so we can run the code AND see your attempt. o it is not the TA’s job to track down why your code doesn’t compile. It IS our job to help you in labs and office hours but that requires you working well before the deadline. • Despite permitting group submissions you are responsible for being able to do all the work yourself. Thus, do not divide and conquer the work and don’t let your partner do any part without you: you won’t have a partner on the exams. I suggest doing it separately and then combining your work afterwards. o o You choose your partner (or work alone) • We drop at least one assignment mark for mistakes and life events. This is to be fair to everyone. o Late assignments will result in a mark of zero as a result. o You can submit multiple times until the deadline so submit early and submit often. o Comment out problem code to make it compile and not be penalized as much. Non-compiling code is a common way to lose a lot of marks. o There are no extensions or do-overs. Please don’t ask for one. • Cheating is tested programmatically (using MOSS and other programs). o Similarly, you and your lab mates should NOT discuss the assignment details to avoid problems. o Since you can work in a pair, there is no reason to cheat. Lab Participation: Labs are mandatory and each week you will practice programming using topics from the lecture. They are also designed so we can help you find the answers and for low-stakes practice. • “Attendance” is taken by submitting your work to the LMS. • A brief introduction video may be provided but the TA is there to help. Please ask questions. • They are designed to be synchronous with a TA available during your schedule time. Working outside the scheduled time may mean you get stuck. • We encourage you to work through problems with other (but not copy each other) • A subset of submissions will be graded on a 5 star (or point) scale each week. o 0 stars = unacceptable work, o 1 star or no evaluation = not tested o 4 stars=Acceptable but can be improved o 5 stars = Excellent o A mark of 1 or more stars means you will get full points for the lab that week. o If a lab submission receives 0 stars (such as submitting a blank file or 2 lines of code), we will audit all previous labs for that student to see if this is a pattern (like a tax audit). o You can expect your labs will be reviewed at least once (probably more) per term. • Each student can miss 2 labs (for life events). There is no need to contact us about missed submissions. • Successfully completing all lab work in a lab is not required. This is meant to be a low-risk environment to prepare for the weekly assignment. You should accomplish as much as you can in the full lab time. If you submit you work (that compiles) after 2 hours, you should get full lab credit. Other Policies • Please use the Brightspace message board or our discord server for general questions, comments and class logistic messages. I won’t answer private emails about non-private things (or I’ll try not to) because I want everyone to have access to the same answers. o This lets you ask a question, get answers faster and ensure everyone gets the same information ensuring fairness. If it only affects you: email away. o • We may adopt discord to let students ask informal questions and to conduct office hours. To join discord you will need to provide your contact information with your discord ID on a form • Office hours are for you; please take advantage of them. • My phone is effectively ornamental. Email, discord, and message board posts are more effective. raw_text: COMS 2003: Theoretical Foundations in Communication and Media Studies Summer, 2025 Location: Brightspace Course Delivery: Online Asynchronous Instructor: Carmen Warner (she/her) Email: carmenwarner@cunet.carleton.ca (please use your Carleton email for all correspondence and put COMS 2003 in subject line) TA: Desirrea Meney TA Email: desirreameney@cmail.carleton.ca Office Hours: On Zoom by appointment Brightspace: https://brightspace.carleton.ca/d2l/home/339349 Prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): COMS 1001 and COMS 1002, and second-year standing in Communication and Media Studies (including BGInS related specializations and streams), or permission of the School of Journalism and Communication. Course Description This course offers students a survey of the major theorists, theoretical traditions, and central debates in Communication and Media Studies. Organized roughly chronologically, each class will cover the historical context of the theorist(s) and theories, the key arguments of said theories, and consider some of their contemporary applications. Students will also have the opportunity to develop critical reading and thinking skills as well as academic writing skills through course assignments and pre-recorded course materials. Land Acknowledgment Carleton University acknowledges the location of its campus on the traditional, unceded territories of the Algonquin nation. If you spend extended time outside of Ottawa, I encourage you to learn more about whose land you are occupying at www.native-land.ca Diversity Statement Carleton University supports an inclusive learning environment where diverse communities and perspectives are recognized and respected. Our goal as a community is to always ensure a safe learning environment that welcomes open and honest dialogue. We do not allow any form of discrimination, including but not limited to those based on color, age, race, religion, disability, gender, gender identity, gender expression and sexual orientation. Faculty and students are expected to commit to creating a learning environment that encourages inquiry and self-expression, while also demonstrating diligence in respecting how other students may have different viewpoints than their own. Learning Objectives By the end of this course students will have a working knowledge of the key theorists, theories, and debates that have shaped critical communication studies throughout the mid-19th and 20th centuries. They will also gain skills to apply these theories to an analysis of our contemporary media landscape. The content of this course along with course assignments and in-class workshops will prepare students for the upper year courses of the Communication and Media Studies undergraduate program. Required Readings All course readings are available on ARES which can be accessed through our course’s Brightspace page. You are expected to complete required readings before watching the related lecture materials to get the most out of the pre-recorded materials. There is no required textbook for this class. Course Format This class is online and asynchronous. There are no required synchronous sessions; however, I recommend you come to office hours when you are able. Asynchronous classes are very different from in-person instruction, and I cannot replicate the experience of in-person teaching. I am available to answer simpler questions throughout the course via email or discuss more complex questions over Zoom during office hours or by appointment. Please note: all materials including readings, recorded lectures, and posted audiovisual materials may be tested. Note on Remote Learning Remote learning requires you to be self-directed in moving through the course content and, especially in a condensed summer course, it is especially important that you stay on top of materials. There are six weeks of material with two topics per week. The material is broken down step-by-step into a schedule of what you should be completing each week of the term. Please reach out to me anytime for help and support. Grade Breakdown Synthesis Assignment 20% Due: Friday, May 16th at 11:59pm Test #1 15% When: 1 hour between May 28th-30th Infographic Assignment 20% Due: Friday, June 6th at 11:59pm Test #2 15% When: 1 hour between June 13th-16th Case Study Assignment 30% Due: Tuesday, June 17th at 11:59pm Synthesis Assignment (20%) The synthesis assignment is a short essay wherein you will demonstrate your understanding of key aspects of Marxism and Frankfurt School Critical Theory (Culture Industry). Your essay should be 4 pages not including citations, double-spaced, follow APA formatting, and have the following structure: -Introduction -Historical context and key arguments of Marxism -Historical context and key arguments of Culture Industry theory -Connect the theories to one another -Conclusion (Why do these theories matter?) While it is okay to cite the lectures, this should be in addition to citing the readings. Properly cited, direct engagement with the readings is expected and necessary to do well on this assignment. Assignments must be submitted on Brightspace by Friday, May 16th at 11:59pm. Test #1 (15%) Test #1 will take place on Brightspace between May 28th and May 30th. Once you start the test, you will have one hour to complete it. Make sure you have the time set aside for the test as you cannot restart it. The test will be a mixture of multiple choice, true/false and short answer questions. While open book, you will not have time to search through lectures and readings for answers, so you should have a firm understanding of course materials before beginning. Infographic Assignment (20%) The Infographic Assignment is a visual representation of either media effects, technological determinism, or semiotics and an application of that theory to a real-world example. Your infographic should: -Demonstrate your understanding of the theory you’ve chosen -Be clearly illustrated through your example -Be visually engaging with appropriate image and style choices -Use text sparingly and effectively Examples of effective infographics will be included in lecture materials prior to the assignment’s due date, Friday, June 6th at 11:59pm. Test #2 (15%) Test #2 will take place on Brightspace between June 13th and June 16th. Once you start the test, you will have one hour to complete it. Make sure you have the time set aside for the test as you cannot restart it. The test will be a mixture of multiple choice, true/false and short answer questions. While open book, you will not have time to search through lectures and readings for answers, so you should have a firm understanding of course materials before beginning. Case Study Assignment (30%) Your final assignment for this course is a case study. The case study is an opportunity for you to connect the theories you have learned to a real-world, empirical case. Tell me about something that interests you! You must engage with 2 theorists from Week 4 – Week 6, though the theorists need not be from the same topic. Your case study should be 5 pages not including citations, double-spaced, follow APA formatting, and have the following structure: -Introduce your case study -Explain the key aspects of the 2 theories you’ve chosen -Connect the theory to the case study -Conclusion (Why does this matter?) While it is okay to cite the lectures, this should be in addition to citing the readings. Properly cited, direct engagement with the readings is expected and necessary to do well on this assignment. Assignments must be submitted on Brightspace by Tuesday, June 17th at 11:59pm. Course Schedule Week 1: May 5-9 Topic 1: Course Introduction What is Theory? What does it mean to study communication critically? Required Reading: Course Syllabus Topic 2: Marxism Required Reading: Auslander, Philip. (2020). “Algorithmic Enclaves: Affective Politics and Algorithms in the Neoliberal Social Media Landscape.” In: M. Boler and E. Davis (eds.), Affective Politics of Digital Media: Propaganda by Other Means (pp. 186-203). New York and London: Routledge. **Test #1 May 28th-30th** Week 5: June 2-6 Topic 1: Political Economy Required Reading: Smythe, Dallas. (1977). “Communications: Blindspot of Western Marxism.” Canadian Journal of Political and Social Theory 1(3): 1-21. McChesney, Robert. (2003). “Theses on Media Deregulation.” Media, Culture & Society 25(1): 125-133. Topic 2: Cultural Studies: Communication as Culturally Embedded Required Reading: Hall, Stuart. (1980). “Encoding/Decoding.” In: S. Hall, D. Hobson, A. Lowe, and P. Willis (eds), Culture, Media, Language: Working Papers in Cultural Studies, 1972-79 (pp. 117-127). London: Routledge. Carey, James. (1989). “A Cultural Approach to Communication.” In: Communication as Culture: Essays on Media and Society (pp. 13-36). Boston: Unwin Hyman. **Infographic Assignment due Friday, June 6th at 11:59pm** Week 6: June 9-13 Topic 1: Media and the Gaze Required Reading: Mulvey, Laura. (1975). “Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema.” Screen 16(1): 6-18. hooks, bell. (1992). “The Oppositional Gaze: Black Female Spectators.” In: Black Looks: Race and Representation (pp. 115-131). Boston: South End Press. Topic 2: Representation: Making Spatial Connections Required Reading: Said, Edward. (1978). “Orientalism.” In: B. Ashcroft (ed.), Post-Colonial Studies Reader (pp. 87-91). London: Routledge. Gilchrist. Kristen. (2010). “‘Newsworthy’ Victims?: Exploring Differences in Canadian Local Press Coverage of Missing/Murdered Aboriginal and White Women.” Feminist Media Studies 10(4): 373-390. **Test #2 June 13th-16th** **Case Study Assignment due Tuesday, June 17th at 11:59pm** Course Policies Submission and Return of Coursework All assignments must be submitted via Brightspace at the time and date indicated on the syllabus and will be returned in a timely manner. No assignments will be accepted via email. Any changes in assignment deadlines will be posted on the course website. All written assignments should be typed, double-spaced, using standard margins, and size-12 font. Upload written work in .doc or .pdf format. Late Assignments Late work will be penalized by one third of a letter grade for each day past the deadline (i.e., a B+ becomes a B after one day, a B- after two days, and so on). No assignments will be accepted one week after the due date. If you require an extension for any reason (life happens!), email me with your request at least 24 hours before the due date. This late policy does not apply to tests which will not be reopened past the deadline. Statement on Student Conduct As part of a learning community, it is our responsibility to contribute to an engaging, inclusive, and safe learning environment. During all class-related activities, please engage in respectful and courteous communication and follow Carleton’s Student Rights and Responsibilities Policy. Harassment of any kind will not be tolerated in this class. Do not cut and paste, screenshot, share course content, or post the words of your classmates, TA, or Instructor outside of class without permission. Students are not permitted to take photographs, screenshots, or record other students, TAs, or instructors unless they obtain explicit permission from the professor and all other students. All work submitted in this course must be uniquely your own. When submitting assignments and/or completing exams, you are expected to articulate responses in your own words rather than cutting and pasting from course materials without permission, which is a form of plagiarism. Communication and Media Studies does not allow students to turn in work that has been submitted for academic credit more than once without permission from their instructors. Examples of unauthorized resubmission of work might include but are not limited to submission of the same paper, written passages, arguments, or ideas submitted for academic credit to another class. Minor changes of phrasing or addition of new written passages to existing work is not enough to constitute new work. Please contact your instructor if there is any question about whether your submission of coursework constitutes a violation of the policy. If it is determined an assignment has been submitted more than once, it will not receive credit. Course Copyright Classroom teaching and learning activities, including lectures, discussions, presentations, etc., by both instructors and students, are copyright protected and remain the intellectual property of their respective author(s). All course materials, including PowerPoint presentations, outlines, and other materials, are also protected by copyright and remain the intellectual property of their respective author(s). Students registered in the course may take notes and make copies of course materials for their own educational use only. Students are not permitted to reproduce or distribute lecture notes and course materials publicly for commercial or non-commercial purposes without express written consent from the copyright holder(s). Declining Online Imaging and Recording Statement This course may include classes or sessions that use video conferencing platforms, such as Zoom and MS Teams. This raises some important privacy considerations. In order to foster an active learning environment where you and your classmates are fully engaged in class, your instructor may prefer that you keep your camera on during class. However, you are not required to turn your camera on and may decide to turn it on or off at any time. Classes or sessions will not be recorded by the instructor. Statement on Plagiarism The University Academic Integrity Policy defines plagiarism as “presenting, whether intentionally or not, the ideas, expression of ideas or work of others as one’s own.” This includes reproducing or paraphrasing portions of someone else’s published or unpublished material, regardless of the source, and presenting these as one’s own without proper citation or reference to the original source. Examples of sources from which the ideas, expressions of ideas or works of others may be drawn from include but are not limited to: books, articles, papers, literary compositions and phrases, performance compositions, chemical compounds, artworks, laboratory reports, research results, calculations and the results of calculations, diagrams, constructions, computer reports, computer code/software, material on the internet and/or conversations. Examples of plagiarism include, but are not limited to: any submission prepared in whole or in part, by someone else, including the unauthorized use of generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT); using ideas or direct, verbatim quotations, paraphrased material, algorithms, formulae, scientific or mathematical concepts, or ideas without appropriate acknowledgment in any academic assignment; using another’s data or research findings without appropriate acknowledgement; submitting a computer program developed in whole or in part by someone else, with or without modifications, as one’s own; and failing to acknowledge sources through the use of proper citations when using another’s work and/or failing to use quotations marks. Plagiarism is a serious offence that cannot be resolved directly by the course’s instructor. The Associate Dean of the Faculty conducts a rigorous investigation, including an interview with the student, when an instructor suspects a piece of work has been plagiarized. Penalties are not trivial. They can include a final grade of “F” for the course. Approval of Final Grades Standing in a course is determined by the course instructor subject to the approval of the Faculty Dean. This means that grades submitted by an instructor may be subject to revision. No grades are final until they have been approved by the Dean. Statement on Student Mental Health: As a student you may experience a range of mental health challenges that significantly impact your academic success and overall well-being. If you need help, please speak to someone. There are numerous resources available both on- and off-campus to support you. For more information, please consult https://wellness.carleton.ca/. Academic Accommodation: Carleton is committed to providing academic accessibility for all individuals. You may need special arrangements to meet your academic obligations during the term. The accommodation request processes, including information about the Academic Consideration Policy for Students in Medical and Other Extenuating Circumstances, are outlined on the Academic Accommodations website (students.carleton.ca/course-outline).

Lauriault, Associate Professor, Critical Media and Big Data, School of Journalism and Communication Contact: Tracey.Lauriault@carleton.ca COMS3403 in the Subject line! Student Hours: Wednesdays, 12:30 – 15:30, RB4110b Course Website: https://brightspace.carleton.ca/d2l/home. Prerequisites: third year standing in Communication and Media Studies (including BPAPM related specializations), or permission of the School of Journalism and Communication. Course Calendar Description Examines the relationship between communication technology and society, including factors that contribute to changes in the collection, storage and distribution of information and their cultural implications. Topics Covered The course examines the contemporary and historical relationship between communication technology, society and culture. Students will study the philosophy of technology from multiple perspectives and will learn to identify and critically deliberate these perspectives including a variety of new and old concepts in the field. Technocolonialism is this year’s them, and student will read a series of real-world case studies about the politics, policies and practices of the deployment and use of communication technologies in refugee camps and in immigration contexts. A variety of technological systems large and small will also be examined and discussed such as infrastructure, platforms, software, applications, smart cities, digital twins, biometrics, etc. COMS3403 1 COMS3403 Culture, Technology & Society Class Time: Thursday’s 08:30-11:30 In Person Lecture: ME3328 Student Hours: Thursday’s, 12:00 – 14:30, RB4110b Learning Outcomes Students will: • learn that the material aspects of the communication infrastructure are a feat of engineering, but also science, society, culture, geography, politics, funding, and ideology. • study old and contemporary technologies as an assemblage of artefacts, networks, systems, institutions, practices, and people. • think critically about technology by engaging with key sociological and philosophical concepts and contextualize these with contemporary examples. • acquire the knowledge and skill to identify and distinguish technological narratives and the vocabulary to describe and articulate them. • write a research paper that includes a case study to objectively study a technological system. Required Textbook(s) Compulsory Textbook Mirca Madianou. 2024. Technocolonialism: When Technology for Good is Harmful. Polity. Cost: E-Book starts @ $30.00, paper copy at the CU Bookstore $45.00. Technology Description & Diary Week 2 Jan.16 @07:00 AM (2 pages + appendix) (10%) Abstain from using a a technology that you carry with you and/or use every day for a 24-hour period. Keep a diary/log of the experience. It can be a device, an app, or anything else - one you think you cannot do without. Abstain from using that technology and keep a diary/log of observations related to that abstention. Add the log/diary as an appendix. In 2-pages discuss the experience. Be sure to name the device or app, name the company that made it, state where it is made and the cost. Also, does it rely on an underlying infrastructure, are there ongoing costs, and to whom do you pay these costs? How often have you replaced it, can it be recycled, and do you have the right to repair it? Is there is a terms of use, privacy policy or licence involved? If data are involved, who owns them and what are your privacy rights, can they be resold? Finally, describe the device aesthetically, instrumentally, and emotionally and discuss how you use it. 3. Community Observation due Week 10 Mar.20 @07:00 (2 pages) (10%) Students attend a meetup, a hackathon, Data Day, or a technology seminar on or off campus. Students attend as observers and will include the following in their observations about the event: name of the event, they type of technology discussed, where it was broadcast or the location it was held, the date attended, indicate the sponsor, convenor, how the event was structured and advertised, the purpose of the event, what is being discussed, how many people attended (downloads/views), what are the demographics of the speakers and participants (age, ancestry background, gender/sex, etc.), what did they do, who is doing most of the talking, who is asking questions, was social media used, what did you learn? What did you gain by attending this event? 5. FINAL RESEARCH ESSAY (40%) – 3 parts Students will write a research essay that includes a case about any technology of their choice deployed in a crisis, a COMS3403 3 COMS3403 Culture, Technology & Society Class Time: Thursday’s 08:30-11:30 In Person Lecture: ME3328 Student Hours: Thursday’s, 12:00 – 14:30, RB4110b humanitarian or refugee or immigration context, or in a refugee camp/management context. Indianapolis: Hackett Publishing Company, Inc. • Learn to use Zotero a free citation software Schedule: Week 1 – Jan. 09 – Introduction - Are technologies political? Welcome to the class! We will get to know each other, go over readings and learning objectives. We start by defining what technology is and explore its meaning by discussing the devices and apps we wear, carry with us and use every day. You will also begin to learn how to frame technology ideologically and to critically discuss whether artifacts have politics. The 1st in-class activity involves using the OpenAi ChatGPT and a small questionnaire. Have a laptop, tablet or your phone, also a pen/pencil! We will also format a document using styles and register everyone to a class GDrive Folder and create a spreadsheet of resources for the class (Agencies in Canada and abroad that support humanitarian efforts). Compulsory Reading: Winner, Langdon (1980) “Do Artifacts Have Politics?” Daedalus, Vol. 109, No. 1, Modern Technology: Problem or Opportunity? Winter, 121-136. Compulsory News Article Readings: Cadwalladr, Carole. “A New Era Dawns. America’s Tech Bros Now Strut Their Stuff in the Corridors of Power.” The Observer, November 10, 2024, sec. Opinion. The Guardian. O’Mara, Margaret. 2019. “Opinion | The Church of Techno-Optimism.” The New York Times, September 28, sec. Opinion. Reference Materials: Barbrook, Richard and Andy Cameron (1996) “The Californian Ideology”, Science as Culture, 6(1) pp.44-72. version here at Mute. Week 2 – Jan. 16 – What is Technology and How to Study it? Students will learn that social and cultural change does not alone determine technological outcomes, nor does technology alone determine social outcomes, instead there are many interrelated factors that shape both in context. We will also discuss what digital humanitarianism. For the 2nd in-class activity we will examine a humanitarian case that uses a communication technology and discuss it considering this week’s reading. We will also look at some statistics and the geography of displacement. Compulsory readings: Madianou (2024) Introduction: Digital Humanitarianism as Technocolonialism, 1-28. Compulsory News Article reading: Riley, Tonya, Meryl Kornfield, Azi Paybarah, Maegan Vazquez, Justine McDaniel, Mariana Alfaro, Hannah Dormido, et al. “Analysis | The Technology 202: Activists Slam Palantir for Its Work with ICE Ahead of Market Debut.” Washington Post, September 29, 2020. COMS3403 6 COMS3403 Culture, Technology & Society Class Time: Thursday’s 08:30-11:30 In Person Lecture: ME3328 Student Hours: Thursday’s, 12:00 – 14:30, RB4110b Resource Material: Quan-Haase, Anabel (2020) Chapter 1 The Technological Society, 1-23. Paillé, Pauline, James Besse, Hampton Toole, Chryssa Politi, Shruti Viswanathan, Eunice Namirembe, Jyoti Nayak, et al. “Emerging Technologies in the Humanitarian Sector: Technology Deep Dive Series.” RAND Corporation, October 17, 2024. Week 3 – Jan. 23 – History and Philosophy of Technology Students will study some of the key theoretical perspectives that frame thinking about technology, society and culture such as: technological determinism, instrumentalism, substantivism, gender and technology, critical theory, a composite view, social constructivism and science and technology studies (STS). The 3rd in-class activity involves discussing procedural fairness in the use of AI as it pertains to Immigration in Canada. Compulsory readings: Quan-Haase, Anabel (2020) o Chapter 2 Technology in Society: A Historical Overview, 24-48. o Chapter 3 Theoretical Perspectives on Technology, 49-70. Compulsory Blog Post Scassa, Teresa. “Procedural Fairness and AI - Some Lessons from a Recent Decision.” TeresaScassa.Ca (blog), October 17, 2022. Refence Material Policies: Roshan, Hassibullah. “Exploring AI-Powered Migration Management in Canada through the Prisms of Algorithmic Governmentality, Technosolutionist, and Critical Data Studies.” Working Paper. Toronto: Toronto Metropolitan University, January 2024. Week 4 – Jan. 30 Making Technology This week students learn about the social shaping qualities of technology, large technological systems, social and technological assemblages and the role of techno-social design, making technology for a purpose and then how to critically reflect on the outcomes and possible impact. The 4th in-class activity will watch a Ted Talk by Joy Buolamwini How I'm fighting bias in AI and discuss the work she did and why she did it and compare that to the work of the Computer Chaos Club in Germany. Compulsory readings: Kubitschko, Sebastian, (2015), Hackers’ media practices: Demonstrating and articulating expertise as interlocking arrangements, Convergence: The International Journal of Research into New Media Technologies 2015, Vol. 21(3) 388–402. Compulsory News Article readings: Refugee Law Initiative. “Balancing Security and Human Rights: AI’s Role in EU Border Management.” Refugee Law Initiative Blog, May 31, 2024. Reference Materials: Qiu, Jack Linchuan (2016) Goodbye ISlave a Manifesto for Digital Abolition. Geopolitics of Information. Urbana: University of Illinois Press. COMS3403 7 COMS3403 Culture, Technology & Society Class Time: Thursday’s 08:30-11:30 In Person Lecture: ME3328 Student Hours: Thursday’s, 12:00 – 14:30, RB4110b Quan-Haase, Anabel (2020) Chapter 4 Techno-Social Designing, 71-93. Quan-Haase, Anabel (2020) Chapter 6 Technology and Inequality, 162-183 and Coded Bias. Film. 7th Empire Media, 2020. Week 5 – Feb. 06 – Adoption and Diffusion of Technology & the Canadian Communication Infrastructure Building on Week 4’s class about large technological systems, this week we examine how technologies are adopted and diffused and what social and technical factors are related to them failing or gaining traction. We connect this to the evolution of the communication infrastructure and the materiality of infrastructure. This week the class is in the Map Library on the 1st floor, and we will do Assignment 3 in the library. Compulsory Reading: Quan-Haase, Anabel (2020) Chapter 5 Adoption and Diffusion of Technological Innovations, 95-117. Kang, Cecilia, (2017) Arctic Ice Melts and a Digital Rush Follows: Global Warming Has Opened Up More Paths for High-Speed Internet Cables, The New York Times, Dec. 3. Dodge, Martin and Rob Kitchin (2001) The Atlas of Cyberspace, Addison & Wesley. Goldsmith, Jack and Wu, Tim. (2006) Chapter 4. Why Geography Matters in Who Controls the Internet? Illusions of a Borderless World, Oxford University Press, p.49-64. Week 6 – Feb. 13 – The Logics of Digital Humanitarianism Between weeks 1 and 5 we discussed several underlying philosophical approaches to the thinking and studying of technology, and whether technologies are political. This week we take this thinking further consider these philosophies and concepts in terms of the different logics of digital humanitarianism, and the rationales used to build and deploy this form of ‘development’. The 5th in-class activity involves a deep reading of the Madianou chapter, and we will do so by applying a social and technological assemblage approach when doing so and we will discuss the UN OCHA Report. Compulsory Readings: Madianou (2024) Chapter 1: The logics of Technocolonialism. OCHA, April 2021. Study Break Feb. 17-21 Week 7 – Feb. 27 – Biometrics In week 5 we examine infrastructure, and during week 6 we studied the logic of technocolonialism, this week we bring these discussions together by examining the infrastructure of biometrics, biosensing and how knowledge is constructed from these systems. The 6th in-class activity. Involves conducting a search of the companies that make biometric systems and assess if the Government of Canada procures any of these technologies. Compulsory Readings: Madianou (2024) Chapter 2: Biometric Infrastructure Compulsory Industry Announcement & News Article: Canada, Office of the Privacy Commissioner of. “News Release: Cadillac Fairview Collected 5 Million Shoppers’ Images,” October 29, 2020. Thales. “Saudi Arabia Biometric Borders.” Industry. Accessed December 19, 2024.
