Biometrics Institute. “Types of Biometrics.” Accessed December 19, 2024. Week 8 – Mar. 06 – Accountability and Transparency The chapter critically discusses the practices of extracting feedback from those who must use a variety of technologies in a refugee and humanitarian context as part of impact assessment. This week we will discuss the meaning of accountability, transparency and impact are in general, and what measures and indicators of success might be in a humanitarian context, as it pertains to technology projects. For the 7th in-class activity we will examine the UNHCR handbooks on impact assessment, and the HHI Playbook to assess the evaluation approaches to technologies discussed in the book. Compulsory Readings: Madianou (2024) Chapter 3: Extracting Data and the Illusion of Accountability, 82-101. Harvard Humanitarian Initiative (HHI). “Technologies in Humanitarian Settings: Engagement and Local Innovation - Playbook.” Harvard Humanitarian Initiative (HHI), December 2022. Resource Materials: IATI Standard. “International Aid Transparency Initiative (IATI).” NGO. Accessed December 19, 2024. International Development Research Centre (IDRC). “Artificial Intelligence for Development,” November 20, 2024. COMS3403 9 COMS3403 Culture, Technology & Society Class Time: Thursday’s 08:30-11:30 In Person Lecture: ME3328 Student Hours: Thursday’s, 12:00 – 14:30, RB4110b United Nations “Accountability | Policy Portal.” Accessed December 19, 2024. https://policy.un.org/en/accountability. United Nations “UN Transparency Protocol.” Accessed December 19, 2024. United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR). “UNHCR Assessment and Monitoring Resource Centre |.” UNHCR Assessment and Monitoring Resource Centre. Accessed December 19, 2024. https://www.unhcr.org/handbooks/assessment/. Week 9 – Mar. 13 – Experimenting Should the technology ethos of ‘fail fast, fail often or fail cheap’ be applied in a humanitarian context? Should the idiom ‘do not look a gift horse in the mouth’ be applied when it comes to technologies that are given away for free or at the cost of trading data in a humanitarian context? And what does it mean to learn by doing and to experiment in a humanitarian context? We will discuss these questions as we examine the reading for this week. The 8th in-class activity involves examining what balanced, principled and complementary partnerships look like. Compulsory Readings: Madianou (2024) Chapter 4: Surreptitious Experimentation Enchantment, Coloniality and Control, 102-127. Compulsory Report: S., Sebastián, Melendéz J., Khan S., and Vinck P. “Technologies in Humanitarian Settings: Balanced, Principled, and Complementary Partnerships.” Harvard Humanitarian Initiative (HHI), 2022. Resource Materials: Sandvik, Kristin Bergtora, Katja Lindskov Jacobsen, and Sean Martin McDonald. “Do No Harm: A Taxonomy of the Challenges of Humanitarian Experimentation.” International Review of the Red Cross 99, no. 904 (April 2017): 319–44. Week 10 – Mar. 20 – Automating Harm and Inequality Technologies to manage aid and people in a humanitarian context are considered to be objective and neutral. This week we revisit this thesis by examining how some technologies that seem to make things easier for the funder may in fact, depending on the algorithm and the systems to govern the data may instead automate bias, harm and inequality. For the 9th in-class activity we will watch an interview by Alexander Heffner with Virgnia Eubanks about her book entitled automating inequality and discuss how the issues the US resemble those discussed by Madianou. We will also explore a code of ethics to managing information in a humanitarian context. Compulsory Readings: Madianou (2024) Chapter 5: The Humanitarian Machine Automating Harm, 129-152. Compulsory Video and Report: PBS - The Open Mind, Automating Inequality, Season 2018. Accessed December 20, 2024. COMS3403 10 COMS3403 Culture, Technology & Society Class Time: Thursday’s 08:30-11:30 In Person Lecture: ME3328 Student Hours: Thursday’s, 12:00 – 14:30, RB4110b Greenwood, Faine, Caitlin Howarth, Danielle Escudero Poole, Nathaniel A. Raymond, and Daniel P. Scarnecchia. “The Signal Code: A Human Rights Approach to Information During Crisis,” 2017. . Resource Materials: O’Neil, Cathy. Weapons of Math Destruction: How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy. First edition. New York: Crown, 2016. Week 11 – Mar. 27 – Resistance There is often the assumption that displaced people, whether as refugees in new host country or living in camps, or perhaps worse on the move are passive recipients of the technologies from aid agencies. This week we look at whether resistance is futile and what acts of resistance might look like. For the 10th in-class activity we will discuss activism, resistance and technological citizenship. Compulsory Readings: Madianou (2024) Chapter 6: Mundane Resistance Contesting Technocolonialism in Everyday Life, 153-178. Compulsory News Article readings: Wells, Amanda. “Digital Refugee Resistance, Power, Representation and Algorithmic Censorship.” Forced Migration Review, May 2024. Taylor, Becky. “Refugee Resistance and the Persistence of ‘Ingratitude.’” History Workshop, February 3, 2021. https://www.historyworkshop.org.uk/migration/refugee-resistance/. Resource Materials: Feenberg, Andrew. “Agency and Citizenship in a Technological Society.” In Spaces for the Future, edited by Joseph C. Pitt and Ashley Shew, 1st ed., 98–107. Routledge, 2017. Week 12 – Apr. 03 – Technocolonialism and Exam Review This week we conclude the readings on technocolonialism and consider what our roles and responsibilities are in the context of humanitarianism and technology. For the 11th in-class activity we will prepare for the final exam. Compulsory Readings: Madianou (2024) Conclusion: Technocolonialism as Infrastructural Violence, 179-198. GenAI The use of generative artificial intelligence tools (e.g. ChatGPT) is not permitted, either generally or for a specific assignment, any use of generative AI tools to produce assessed content is considered a violation of academic integrity standards. Plagiarism: Plagiarism is “presenting, whether intentionally or not, the ideas, expression of ideas or work of others as one’s own”. raw_text: COMS 3410 Visual Media and Communication Communication and Media Studies Fall 2025 Course Delivery: In person Course page: https://brightspace.carleton.ca/d2l/home/369402 Prerequisites and Preclusions: third year standing in Communication and Media Studies or permission of the School of Journalism and Communication. Location: Mackenzie Building 3328 Time: Thursday 6:05 pm - 8:30 pm Professor Siobhan Angus (she/her) [You can call me Siobhan] Office Location: 4203 Richcraft Hall Office Hours: By appointment, book here. Email: siobhan.angus@carleton.ca (Please use your Carleton University e-mail account to contact me so the email doesn’t get flagged as spam.) Teaching Assistant: Mikaila Cober Email: MikailaCober@cmail.carleton.ca All course related emails should come to the instructor, not the TA. 1 COURSE DESCRIPTION In today’s image-saturated world, visuals shape our perceptions, communication, and realities more than ever before. In fact, it’s estimated that more photographs are taken every two minutes in the U.S. than were taken globally in the entire 19th century. But what does this constant bombardment of images mean for how we understand and navigate the world? This course delves into the complex role of visual production in contemporary society, examining a wide range of media from painting and photography to television, social media, and emerging digital technologies. Through critical discussions and analysis, we will explore how visual culture intersects with politics, power, and the creation of knowledge. By the end of the course, you’ll be equipped to decode the visual language that surrounds us and understand its profound impact on culture, identity, and society. This class includes experiential learning and there will be field trips to the Carleton University Art Gallery, the Museum of Nature, Canadian Museum of History, and the National Gallery of Canada. There are no costs associated with admission, but you are responsible for arranging transportation. Carleton University acknowledges the location of its campus on the traditional, unceded territories of the Algonquin nation. LAND ACKNOWLEDGMENT DIVERSITY STATEMENT Carleton University supports an inclusive learning environment where diverse communities and perspectives are recognized and respected. Our goal as a community is to always ensure a safe learning environment that welcomes open and honest dialogue. We do not allow any form of discrimination, including but not limited to those based on color, age, race, religion, disability, gender, gender identity, gender expression and sexual orientation. Faculty and students are expected to commit to creating a learning environment that encourages inquiry and self- expression, while also demonstrating diligence in respecting how other students may have different viewpoints than their own. COURSE SPECIFICS Learning Outcomes By the end of this class, students should be able to 1. Identify: major figures and comprehend keywords in the field of visual culture studies. 2. Analyze: apply formal analysis and critical visual thinking to works of art using appropriate vocabulary. 3. Contextualize: evaluate artworks in terms of their historical and contemporary political contexts, as well as their rhetoric, use of evidence, and interpretations. 4. Engage: learn how to use library resources, exhibitions, and special collections on campus and across Ottawa for your own research and nurture your lifelong engagement in the arts as well as engaging in dialogue around key issues in visual culture studies by contributing to a collaborative classroom environment. COMS 3410 2 Texts & Course Materials All readings will be available for download from Brightspace. There is no assigned textbook or costs associated with the readings. EVALUATION Grade Breakdown Reflection Papers Workshops Reading Annotations Active Engagement 51% 15% 20% 14% Reflection Papers Over the course of the semester, you will submit three reflection papers. There are four options, but you are asked to choose three. Do not do all four. You can choose the assignments that work the best with your schedule and align with your interests. These papers intend to encourage reflection over time to inspire growth in critical analysis and writing skills. Each paper is worth 17% of your final mark, for a total of 51%. They are due before class on the day of submission and must be uploaded to Brightspace by 6:05 pm. Each reflection should be two to three pages in length, typed and double-spaced. The assignments are tied to the weekly lectures, readings, and Field Trips, with detailed instructions for each reflection posted on Brightspace. Reflection Paper #1: The Gaze (due by 6:05 pm on October 16) Reflection Paper #2: Visual Analysis (due by 6:05 pm on October 30) Reflection Paper #3: The Science of Seeing (due by 6:05 pm on November 20) Reflection Paper #4: Constructing History (due by 6:05 pm on December 4) Workshops Over the course of the semester, you will participate in at least three workshops and submit short reflections after. There are four options, but you are asked to choose three. If you complete all four, you will receive a bonus of 1% on the total of your final course grade. If you miss two or more workshops, you can do a make-up workshop the week of November 27. Full instructions are posted to Brightspace. I strongly recommend you aim to complete the in-class workshops. Each workshop is worth 5%, up to a total of 15% Workshop #1: Close Looking (in class on September 25) Workshop #2: The Gaze (in class on October 2) Workshop #3: Branding, Consumer Culture, and Visual Capitalism (in class on October 30) Workshop #4: Visual Activism (in class on Nov 14, 2024) COMS 3410 3 Reading annotations Five times this semester, I will ask you to annotate, that is, to make notes on the course readings. To do this, you will use Perusall, which is a digital annotation platform that allows us to collectively annotate readings. Each week that you annotate, you need to make at least three substantive annotations for full grades. On weeks with multiple readings, the annotations can be limited to one reading or spread across the readings. Perusall is an online platform that allows for group annotations of course readings. You login at http://perusall.com. You will need to create an account (Accounts on Perusall are free). Please use your full name for your account. Once you log in, use the course code ANGUS-722KA or this link: https://app.perusall.com/join/angus-722ka If you are not comfortable using a third-party platform, you may submit reading responses instead. They should be 400-500 words and substantively analyze key issues in the readings, rather than providing a summary. If you choose this option, please email them to me before class on the day the readings are assigned. Each week is worth 4%, up to a total of 20% This assignment cannot be completed late. Active Engagement Attendance at all class meetings is necessary and students are expected to come prepared to actively participate in discussion by completing the required reading. Your engagement grade (14%) will reflect various ways you might contribute to the learning environment in and beyond our classroom. People have different comfort levels with speaking in class and there are different ways to contribute to a vibrant and respectful classroom environment. If you are not comfortable speaking in large class discussion, that is ok! Engagement takes many forms. Active Engagement includes: 1. Preparation (reviewing readings and materials before class) 2. Focus (avoiding distractions during class) 3. Presence (Engaged and responsive during small group activities) 4. Asking Questions (in class, over email, in office hours) 5. Listening (paying attention to what other people are saying) 6. Specificity (referring to specific ideas from readings and discussions) 7. Synthesizing (making connections between readings and discussions) Everyone is allowed to miss two classes with no penalty without it impacting your active engagement grade. If you are concerned that your engagement might be less visible, you can submit a short report to Brightspace by the end of the term that summarizes how you actively engaged in class this term. This is optional. STATEMENT ON STUDENT CONDUCT As part of a learning community, it is our responsibility to contribute to an engaging, inclusive, and safe learning environment. During all class-related activities, please engage in respectful and courteous communication and follow Carleton’s Student Rights and Responsibilities Policy. Harassment of any kind will not be tolerated in this class. Do not cut and paste, screenshot, share course content, or post the words of your classmates, TA, or Instructor outside of class without permission. Students are not permitted to take photographs, screenshots, or record other students, TAs, or instructors unless they obtain explicit permission from the professor and all other students. All work submitted in this course must be uniquely your own. When submitting assignments and/or completing exams, you are expected to articulate responses in your own words rather than cutting and pasting from course materials without permission. Communication and Media Studies does not allow students to turn in work that has been submitted for academic credit more than once without permission from their instructors. Examples of unauthorized resubmission of work might include but are not limited to submission COMS 3410 7 of the same paper, written passages, arguments, or ideas submitted for academic credit to another class. Minor changes of phrasing or addition of new written passages to existing work is not enough to constitute new work. Please contact your instructor if there is any question about whether your submission of coursework constitutes a violation of the policy. If it is determined an assignment has been submitted more than once, it will not receive credit. COURSE COPYRIGHT Classroom teaching and learning activities, including lectures, discussions, presentations, etc., by both instructors and students, are copyright protected and remain the intellectual property of their respective author(s). All course materials, including PowerPoint presentations, outlines, and other materials, are also protected by copyright and remain the intellectual property of their respective author(s). Students registered in the course may take notes and make copies of course materials for their own educational use only. Students are not permitted to reproduce or distribute lecture notes and course materials publicly for commercial or non-commercial purposes without express written consent from the copyright holder(s). STATEMENT ON PLAGIARISM The University Academic Integrity Policy defines plagiarism as “presenting, whether intentionally or not, the ideas, expression of ideas or work of others as one’s own.” This includes reproducing or paraphrasing portions of someone else’s published or unpublished material, regardless of the source, and presenting these as one’s own without proper citation or reference to the original source. Examples of sources from which the ideas, expressions of ideas or works of others may be drawn from include but are not limited to: books, articles, papers, literary compositions and phrases, performance compositions, chemical compounds, artworks, laboratory reports, research results, calculations and the results of calculations, diagrams, constructions, computer reports, computer code/software, material on the internet and/or conversations. Examples of plagiarism include, but are not limited to: • any submission prepared in whole or in part, by someone else, including the unauthorized use of generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT); • using ideas or direct, verbatim quotations, paraphrased material, algorithms, formulae, scientific or mathematical concepts, or ideas without appropriate acknowledgment in any academic assignment; • using another’s data or research findings without appropriate acknowledgement; • submitting a computer program developed in whole or in part by someone else, with or without modifications, as one’s own; and failing to acknowledge sources through the use of proper citations when using another’s work and/or failing to use quotations marks. • Plagiarism is a serious offence that cannot be resolved directly by the course’s instructor. The Associate Dean of the Faculty conducts a rigorous investigation, including an interview with the student, when an instructor suspects a piece of work has been plagiarized. Penalties are not trivial. They can include a final grade of “F” for the course. COMS 3410 8 APPROVAL OF FINAL GRADES Standing in a course is determined by the course instructor subject to the approval of the Faculty Dean. This means that grades submitted by the instructor may be subject to revision. No grades are final until they have been approved by the Dean. STATEMENT ON STUDENT MENTAL HEALTH As a student you may experience a range of mental health challenges that significantly impact your academic success and overall well-being. raw_text: IRM/OSS 4005A Introduction to Deep Learning Course Outline, Fall 2024 Instructor: Dr. Jie Gao Email: jie.gao6@carleton.ca Lectures: Tuesdays and Thursdays, 10:05 am to 11:25 am, See Carleton Central for the location. Teaching Assistants and Office hours: Information will be posted on Brightspace. Course Calendar Description: Introduction to classification and regression. Optimization, vectorization, gradient descent, cost, loss and activation functions. Introduction and basics to AI, Artificial Neural Networks, forward and backward propagation, Multi-layer Perceptron, and other types of Deep Neural Network models, their applications in multimedia, networks, finance, etc. Includes: Experiential Learning Activity. Prerequisite(s): BIT 2000 and BIT 2400. Course Objectives/Learning Outcomes: The goal of the course is to teach undergraduate students the fundamentals of deep learning. By the end of the course, students should be able to: 1. Understand the basic concept of machine learning. 2. Explain the relation between deep learning and machine learning. 3. Describe applications and trends of deep learning. 4. Become familiar with the mathematical foundation (probabilities, linear algebra, optimization) of deep learning. 5. Build and train basic deep neural networks using libraries. 6. Explain important concepts such as capacity, overfitting, underfitting. 7. Understand ML and MAP estimators, bias, and variance. 8. Understand and apply stochastic gradient descent and back propagation. 9. Describe the basic ideas of common supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms (logistic regression, SVM, KNN, decision trees, PCA, 𝑘-means) 10. Understand regularization for deep learning. Textbook/Equipment/Material: Main textbook: Ian Goodfellow, Bengio Yoshua, and Aaron Courville. Deep Learning. MIT press (2016). Recommended but not mandatory. Grading Scheme: Grading Items Assignments Quizzes Mid-term exam Course project presentation Course project report Participation /Attendance Total Details 3 (practical) + 2 (theoretical) 5 (lowest grade dropped) Oct. 17 (in class) Nov. 26, Nov. 28, Dec. 3, Dec. 5 Dec. 6 5 Randomly selected classes % 15 20 30 15 15 5 100 Note: There will be 5 Assignments, each weighing 3%; 5 in-class Quizzes, each taking 10-15 minutes and weighing 5%, and 4 out of the 5 with the highest grades will be counted into the overall grade; one group project involving a presentation (15%) and a short report (15%), weighing 30% in total. Course Policies: • Academic integrity: Academic important. See Carleton’s policy at https://carleton.ca/registrar/academic-integrity/. Examples of integrity violations include but are not limited to the following: is extremely integrity o present ideas, expression of ideas, or work of others or generated by any form of AI (see below) as one’s own in any grading item (assignment, exam, lab, etc.); o sign attendance for a classmate who is not present in class, or ask another student to sign attendance for you when you are not present in class; o communicate with others, help others, or seek help from others during a quiz or an exam; o use materials or references beyond what is allowed in a quiz or an exam. If a student violates the integrity code in a grading item, the minimum penalty is receiving a 0 for the entire grading item. For example, if a student is found to have signed an attendance for another student who did not attend class, both will receive 0 out of the 5 attendance points. Similarly, if a student uses the solution generated by AI or found on the Internet in any part of an assignment, the student will receive 0 for the entire assignment. The instructor may report an integrity violation, either right away or later in the course, in accordance with Carleton University’s integrity policy: https://carleton.ca/secretariat/wp- content/uploads/Academic-Integrity-Policy-2021.pdf • Generative AI: the use of generative AI as a tool to search for information and assist learning (e.g., by asking AI to clarifying specific content) is allowed, provided that the information generated by the AI is not directly used for gaining grade points in any grading items. However, note that information generated by AI may be misleading or even incorrect. Using content generated by AI as one’s own answers to any questions in any grading item is an integrity violation, regardless of whether the answer is correct or not. • Teaching mode. This is an in-person course. Requests to attend lectures online will not be approved and may not be responded to. The instructor may record himself during lectures for the purpose of enhancing the course content and teaching methods in the future. Access to the recordings will be limited to the instructor. • Video/audio recording. Unless approved by Carleton PMC, any audio/video recording of the lectures must be notified to and approved by the instructor BEFOREHAND. • Video recording of quizzes and exams. To ensure academic integrity, all quizzes and exams may be video recorded. The recording will be deleted by the end of the term if no integrity violations are identified. • Attendance: I will take attendance in randomly chosen classes, each corresponding to 1 attendance point. A missed attendance will receive a grade of 0 unless pre-approved due to medical or other valid reasons recognized by Carleton University policies. Signing an attendance for another person or asking another person to sign attendance are both serious offenses and will lead to 0 in all (not just the one) attendance grades for both students. • • Assignment/report submission: All submissions should be made to the Brightspace so that our course TAs can access them. Submissions via email will not be accepted unless pre-approved for valid special occasions. Late assignments: Late assignments are penalized by 20% each day and will no longer be accepted in any of the following cases: 1) the assignment is submitted after the solution to it has been posted; 2) the penalty exceeds 60% (late by more than 3 days); or 3) the assignment is submitted past the last class day. • Missed exams, assignments, or quizzes: will be counted as absences and receive 0 points unless preapproved. There will be no make-up for missed quizzes or assignments. If absence in a quiz is preapproved due to medical or other valid reasons, the weight of the missed quiz may be moved to other quizzes, and a rescaling may apply to the moved weights. Makeup for a missed final exam (i.e., a deferral) is allowed only if approved by the Registrar’s office. In the case of preapproved absence in a mid-term exam, there will be no makeup, and the weight of the midterm exam may be moved to other grading items. However, if the student wishes, a missed midterm exam may be written and evaluated for feedback purposes only. • Accommodation: Please remind me by email if you are registered with the PMC so that I can upload exams and schedule your exams with them early. If you are not registered with the PMC and require accommodation for any grading item, please note that you will need to complete the academic consideration form with the Registrar’s Office at https://carleton.ca/registrar/academic-consideration- coursework-form/. Note that the form must be submitted before (the deadline of) the corresponding grading item (e.g., the beginning of a quiz). Completing this form does not guarantee accommodation. • Exam: For in-person closed-book exams, one double-sided letter-size hand-written note sheet is allowed. Cell phones, iPads/tablets, headphones, or smartwatches are not allowed during the exams, unless explicitly notified. Calculators are allowed. • Copyright of course materials. The instructor reserves all rights to the course materials, including but not limited to the lecture slides, assignments, assignment solutions, quizzes, and exams. All materials provided throughout the course are meant ONLY for students registered in this course and are NOT to be reproduced or redistributed outside the intended audience online or offline in any way. • Communication: Course-related announcements (assignment postings, exam information, etc.) will be made on the course Brightspace page, which will be automatically sent to your university email (cmail). Please check your Brightspace settings to make sure you can receive these announcements. Outside of my office hours, the preferred method of communication is email. You can send me an email (jie.gao6@carleton.ca) from your cmail at any time, and I will attempt to respond to you within 24 hours. If you have not received a reply from me within 24 hours, please resend the email. I may not respond to an email in rare cases, such as when a student makes a request for something that violates the course policy or cause unfairness to others in the course. If I need to contact you outside of class, I will use your cmail and expect that you will read and respond to this communication in a timely manner. Due to the FIPPA (Freedom of Information & Protection of Privacy) rules, I am unable to communicate with students at a non-Carleton supported email (e.g. Gmail).
